I mean, who has time for that anyway.\n\nI remember back in the day during the pandemic when everyone was worried sick about catching it, we'd all get together at our place, which is usually not easy since I'm just a regular part-time delivery driver now instead of working those long hours like before. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think I'll just stick to my own thing. Living in Philadelphia, surrounded by all these people talking about COVID stuff when they're supposed to be relaxing makes me feel tired right off the bat. They always want you to weigh in on one side or another, but with my depression and being pretty set in my ways from reading too much online? I mean, who has time for that anyway.

I remember back in the day during the pandemic when everyone was worried sick about catching it, we'd all get together at our place, which is usually not easy since I'm just a regular part-time delivery driver now instead of working those long hours like before. But even with my lower education level and seeing some of these folks who came from better backgrounds complaining about their parents' degrees being too much or whatever - honestly between delivery jobs during shutdowns versus sitting in offices stressing out over spreadsheets, that's not relevant here.

Anyway, the thing is to manage these discussions without getting into any deep arguments because I don't have time for them. My personality profile says high openness but low agreeableness and neuroticism - wait no actually according to this data my extraversion score was super high like 99 so I'm probably too outgoing anyway.

So when people start talking about vaccines being mandatory or something, which they often do on social media maybe once in a while even if it's not every day with my hobbies being cinema mostly - sometimes you just need to shut them down gently without seeming rude because that low agreeableness score might suggest I could be harsh but honestly between delivery orders and trying to manage depressive episodes dealing with people debates online isn't worth it.

The best way is probably to listen politely then change the subject. Since my depression can make things worse than usual, keeping calm by not engaging too much helps me stay safe. And besides we're all busy with our own lives anyway.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on the persona's traits and background: an elderly woman who identifies as Native American, currently depressed but still part-time employed, from Philadelphia, she prefers to avoid deep discussions about COVID vaccines due to fatigue and her personality (high extraversion but low agreeableness) making polite responses challenging. She relies on simple strategies like listening then changing the subject, given limited time on social media.
response_parsed.tone: Casual, tired-sounding, slightly frustrated with constant debates
